name: tunnel
context: Open SSH port forwards for local access, reverse access, or SOCKS proxying
info: This skill packages SSH forwarding for the three modes agents actually need: local forwarding with `-L` to reach remote services from the local machine, remote forwarding with `-R` to expose a local service through a remote host, and dynamic forwarding with `-D` to create a SOCKS proxy. It also covers `-N`, `-f`, `ExitOnForwardFailure`, keepalives, bastions, and the difference between TCP bind addresses and target destinations. Use it whenever a service is reachable over SSH but not directly exposed on the network.
triggers: make a socks proxy with ssh
access a private database over ssh
body:
# SSH Port Forwarding

Port forwarding turns an SSH connection into a transport for other services. The shell is optional; the goal is usually to reach a database, admin UI, local dev server, or arbitrary TCP destination through a trusted SSH path.

## Forwarding Modes

- [[ssh/tunnel/local-forward]] with `-L` listens locally and forwards to a destination from the remote side.
- [[ssh/tunnel/remote-forward]] with `-R` listens remotely and forwards back to a destination from the client side.
- [[ssh/tunnel/dynamic-forward]] with `-D` creates a SOCKS proxy on the client side.

## Core Patterns

```bash
ssh -L 5433:127.0.0.1:5432 ops@db-gateway.example.com -N
ssh -R 8080:127.0.0.1:3000 ops@bastion.example.com -N
ssh -D 1080 ops@bastion.example.com -N
```

`-N` avoids running a shell and is the normal mode for tunnels.

## Backgrounding Safely

```bash
ssh -f -n -o ExitOnForwardFailure=yes -L 5433:127.0.0.1:5432 ops@db-gateway.example.com -N
```

Use `ExitOnForwardFailure=yes` so the process only backgrounds after the forward is actually established. Keepalive options from [[ssh/config]] help long-lived tunnels survive transient network changes.

sections.title: Local forwarding with -L
sections.body: `ssh -L local_port:dest_host:dest_port host -N` listens on the client and forwards through the SSH session to `dest_host:dest_port` as seen from the remote side. This is the standard pattern for databases, internal dashboards, and APIs hidden behind a bastion. See [[ssh/tunnel/local-forward]].

sections.title: Remote forwarding with -R
sections.body: `ssh -R remote_port:dest_host:dest_port host -N` makes the remote side listen and send traffic back through the SSH session toward the client side. Use it for reverse tunnels, webhooks into a local dev service, or publishing a private local process through a reachable remote host. See [[ssh/tunnel/remote-forward]].

sections.title: Dynamic forwarding with -D
sections.body: `ssh -D local_port host -N` creates a SOCKS 4/5 proxy that lets client software choose arbitrary destinations through the SSH path. This is useful for browser testing, ad hoc network reach, and traffic inspection. See [[ssh/tunnel/dynamic-forward]].

scripts.name: Open SSH tunnel
scripts.description: Open a local, remote, or dynamic port forward with ssh

SSH_TUNNEL_MODE.description: Tunnel mode: local, remote, or dynamic

SSH_HOST.description: Remote SSH host that anchors the tunnel

SSH_PROXY_JUMP.description: Jump host or chain passed to -J

SSH_BIND_ADDRESS.description: Optional listening bind address for the forward

SSH_LOCAL_PORT.description: Local listening port for local or dynamic forwarding

SSH_REMOTE_PORT.description: Remote listening port for remote forwarding

SSH_DEST_HOST.description: Destination host reached from the opposite side of the tunnel

SSH_BACKGROUND.description: Set to true to background the tunnel with -f -n

SSH_SERVER_ALIVE_COUNT_MAX.description: Number of missed keepalives before disconnect

SSH_EXTRA_ARGS.description: Extra ssh arguments, one per line

localPort.description: Local listening port for local or dynamic forwarding

remotePort.description: Remote listening port for remote forwarding

destinationHost.description: Target host reached through the tunnel

destinationPort.description: Target TCP port reached through the tunnel

forwardSpec.description: Final forwarding specification passed to ssh

depends.reason: Tunnels rely on the same client auth, bastion, and trust workflow as regular SSH sessions
